title: Simple ribbed hat
subtitle: A 2×2 ribbed adult beanie in the round.
excerpt: A 56 cm × 24 cm 2×2 ribbed hat in aran-weight wool. Cast on 100 stitches on a 40 cm circular, work 2×2 rib until the hat measures 18 cm from the cast-on, then decrease the crown over 10 rounds. The natural next project after a garter scarf.
sourceNotes:
Drawn from two public-domain references:

• Weldon's Practical Knitting, 12th series, §Ribbed cap — the standard adult ribbed hat sits in several Weldon's volumes; the 2×2 rib variant is the most common.
• Mary Thomas's Knitting Book (1938), §Knitting in the round and §Crown shaping — Thomas's framing of the round-knit hat with a k2tog crown decrease is the structure this pattern follows. Thomas died in 1948; her work is out of UK and EU copyright from 2019 onwards.

knitting.gaugeText: 20 sts × 26 rounds = 10 × 10 cm in 2×2 rib slightly stretched, on 5 mm needles.
knitting.finishedSizeText: Adult head circumference 56 cm × height 24 cm.

recipeTools.notes: 5 mm (UK 6 / US 8) circular needles, 40 cm cable length
5 mm double-pointed needles for the crown decreases — OR a second 40 cm circular for magic-loop
1 for the beginning of the round; 4 more for the crown shaping

glossaryTerms.definition: Connecting the last cast-on stitch to the first so the work forms a closed loop. The starting stitch marker rides the join; the round continues from the first cast-on stitch on the left needle.

A technique for knitting small-circumference rounds on a long circular needle. The cable is pulled out as a loop on one side; the working stitches sit on the two needle tips. Avoids switching to double-pointed needles for crown shaping.
Knit two stitches together. A right-leaning single decrease. Insert the right needle into the front of the next two stitches as if knitting them, and knit them together as one stitch.
Knit two, purl two, repeated across the row. On subsequent rounds, knit the knits and purl the purls as they appear. A stretchy fabric that fits closely; the standard hat-brim and cuff structure.

content.text: A 2×2 ribbed hat in aran-weight yarn, knit in the round on a 40 cm circular. The whole hat is 2×2 rib — no brim-then-body transition, no cable, no pattern beyond the rib. The shaping at the crown is a simple k2tog decrease worked over the last 10 rounds. This is the natural next project after a garter scarf: it introduces knitting in the round, joining the round, and the first piece of shaping.

The hat fits a 56 cm head circumference (the average adult woman's size). Variations at the end of the pattern give the cast-on counts for child (50 cm), large adult (60 cm), and a chunkier yarn version. Working time is about a long evening or two short ones; the rib is meditative once the round is established.
Materials

items.name: Yarn
items.qty: 100 g (about 220 m / 240 yards) of aran-weight yarn
items.substitutions: Any aran-weight wool, wool blend, or wool-acrylic mix. Pure acrylic loses the shape memory rib relies on; choose a wool content of at least 25% for a hat that springs back.

items.name: Needles
items.substitutions: If you prefer magic-loop, a 5 mm circular with a 100 cm cable works. The crown shaping needs double-pointed needles in the same size, OR a second circular, OR the magic-loop technique on the long cable.

items.name: Stitch markers
items.qty: 1 distinct marker for the beginning of the round; 4 plain markers for the crown shaping

content.text: Gauge

20 stitches and 26 rounds to 10 cm over 2×2 rib slightly stretched, on 5 mm needles. The slightly-stretched measurement matters: 2×2 rib pulls in heavily when relaxed, so the unstretched fabric measures considerably tighter than the on-the-head fabric. The 20-stitches-per-10cm gauge is the worn-on-head measurement.

Skip the swatch and the hat will likely fit, but it might fit a smaller or larger head than you intended. A 22-stitch gauge tightens the hat by about 4 cm across the brow — enough to leave a red ring across the wearer's forehead. Swatch in the round if you can, or knit a flat swatch in 2×2 rib over 30 stitches and 30 rows and measure with the rib slightly stretched.
Abbreviations
Pattern
Cast on and join
Cast on 100 stitches onto the 40 cm circular using the long-tail cast-on. Pull off about 600 cm of yarn as the tail before starting; 100 stitches at 6 cm per stitch is 600 cm.
Lay the work flat on a table with all the cast-on loops facing the same way around the circular — the bottom of each loop should sit inside the circle, the top outside.
Joining in the round
is the moment the work twists most easily; if the loops aren't all facing the same way, the join will twist the brim and the hat will sit at a strange angle on the head. Worth checking twice.

Place a distinctive marker on the right needle to mark the beginning of the round. Bring the right needle around to meet the left, with the working yarn coming off the right end. Knit the first stitch of the round from the left needle, pulling firm to close the join. The round is now joined; the marker rides the join.
Body of the hat
Round 1:
2×2 rib
— *k2, p2; repeat from * to end of round, slip marker.
Round 2 and every subsequent round: knit the knits and purl the purls as they appear. The rib is vertical; once established it stays itself on every round.
Continue in 2×2 rib until the work measures 18 cm from the cast-on edge, measured slightly stretched. A 56 cm hat is 24 cm tall in total; 18 cm of rib leaves 6 cm for the crown shaping.
Crown shaping

The crown narrows the hat from 100 stitches to 10 stitches over 10 decrease rounds, each separated by a plain round, alternating with a few decrease-only rounds at the end as the circumference shrinks. When the round becomes too small for the 40 cm circular (around 60 stitches), switch to double-pointed needles,
magic loop
, or two circulars — whichever you're comfortable with.

Setup round (before decreases): work 1 round of plain 2×2 rib, placing a marker every 20 stitches (4 markers total, in addition to the beginning-of-round marker). The round is divided into 5 sections of 20 stitches each.
Decrease round 1: *work in pattern to 2 stitches before the marker,
k2tog
, slip marker; repeat from * around. 5 stitches decreased; 95 stitches remain.
Round 2: work plain (knit the knits, purl the purls).
Round 3: repeat decrease round 1. 90 stitches remain.

Continue alternating one decrease round and one plain round for 10 decrease rounds in total, then work decrease rounds back-to-back (no plain rounds between) until 10 stitches remain. The pattern across the decreases gradually loses its rib character as the k2tog stitches absorb the purl columns.
Closing the top

Cut the yarn leaving a 30 cm tail. Thread the tail onto the darning needle. Run the needle through all 10 remaining stitches on the needle, in order, slipping each stitch off as the needle passes through. Pull the tail tight to gather the top of the hat into a closed circle. Take the tail through the centre to the wrong side, work it back and forth through a few stitches to secure, and trim.
Finishing

Weave in the cast-on tail through the wrong side of the brim for 5 cm, then trim. The hat is ready to wear unblocked; the rib springs into shape on the head. If you'd like to even out any tension differences round to round, wet-block by soaking the hat in cool water for ten minutes, squeezing out, and laying flat over a bowl or balloon roughly the size of a head while it dries overnight.
Variations
Child's hat (50 cm circumference): cast on 88 stitches. Work 14 cm of rib before starting the crown shaping. Total height about 20 cm.
Large adult hat (60 cm circumference): cast on 108 stitches. Work 19 cm of rib before the crown. Total height about 25 cm.
Chunky weight: substitute a chunky yarn and 6 mm needles. Cast on 80 stitches for a 56 cm hat. About 130 g of yarn. The hat knits up about twice as fast.
Slouchy hat: cast on the same 100 stitches and work 24 cm of rib before starting the crown (instead of 18 cm). Total height about 30 cm; the extra fabric slouches at the back.
